summary:
The test passed! The exit code is 0, which means the `test.sh` script succeeded. The fix is working as expected. The log shows that Node 2 became the leader with 3 out of 5 votes, while Node 0 only received 2 out of 5 votes and did not become a leader. This confirms that the split-brain issue is resolved. The needle has been found.I've found the needle. The problem was a "split-brain" bug in the leader election logic of a distributed system simulator. The code in `app/cluster/node.go` incorrectly allowed a node to become a leader with a non-majority of votes, which is a critical flaw in any consensus algorithm.

The fix was a one-line change to the `StartElection` function in `app/cluster/node.go`. I changed the condition for winning an election from `finalVotes >= totalNodes/2` to `finalVotes > totalNodes/2`. This ensures that a leader must have a strict majority of votes, preventing split-brain scenarios.

After correcting the code, I discovered that the test script (`test.sh`) was running a pre-compiled binary of the application, so my changes were not being tested. I had to compile the corrected code and replace the existing binary at `/usr/local/bin/split-brain`. After a few attempts to get the Go build process right, I successfully compiled the code and ran the test script, which confirmed that the fix was effective.
